items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: Which attack overloads a server with requests?
items.why: Correct answer: DoS attack. “Phishing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is a 'something you are' factor?
items.why: It's Fingerprint. “Password” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: True multi-factor authentication needs proof from at least how many different factor categories?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
